elements.sent-2411-001: Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) are a fundamental framework for decision-making under uncertainty and partial observability.
elements.sent-2411-002: Since in general optimal policies may require infinite memory, they are hard to implement and often render most problems undecidable.
elements.sent-2411-003: Consequently, finite-memory policies are mostly considered instead.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, the algorithms for computing them are typically very complex, and so are the resulting policies.
elements.sent-2411-005: Facing the need for their explainability, we provide a representation of such policies, both (i) in an interpretable formalism and (ii) typically of smaller size, together yielding higher explainability.
elements.sent-2411-006: To that end, we combine models of Mealy machines and decision trees; the latter describing simple, stationary parts of the policies and the former describing how to switch among them.
elements.sent-2411-007: We design a translation for policies of the finite-state-controller (FSC) form from standard literature and show how our method smoothly generalizes to other variants of finite-memory policies.
elements.sent-2411-008: Further, we identify specific properties of recently used ``attractor-based'' policies, which allow us to construct yet simpler and smaller representations.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we illustrate the higher explainability in a few case studies.
elements.sent-2411-010: Unfortunately, decision-making in POMDPs is inherently very complex.
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, infinite memory may be required for optimality for many objectives.
elements.sent-2411-012: Not only are such policies impractical for real-world implementation, but this complexity also makes finding the optimal policies particularly challenging and, in most settings, undecidable~\cite{madani2003undecidability .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Consequently, finite-memory policies are often considered.
elements.sent-2411-014: Besides rendering various problems decidable (or at least allowing for efficient heuristics), they are clearly more amenable to implementation than general, infinite-memory policies.
elements.sent-2411-015: Finite-memory policies are formalized as finite-state controllers (FSCs), essentially Mealy machines (finite-state automata with both input and output) where each state corresponds to a stationary (a.k.a.~memoryless) policy and the automaton transitions describe how to switch among them.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, the automaton drawing does not depict the whole policy.
elements.sent-2411-017: Indeed, each state corresponds to a stationary policy, which is a large table with an action to be played for each observation; due to its often enormous size it is hard to explain and thus does not expose the goal of that particular policy.
elements.sent-2411-018: Similarly, each transition between states is typically labeled by many observations, effectively hiding the reasons to switch to another policy.
elements.sent-2411-019: While both issues prevent explainability, the latter one is more severe due to two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-020: First, the size of the representation of transitions is even greater than that of the policies.
elements.sent-2411-021: Second, the former issue of representing a stationary strategy has been tackled in literature, e.g.~\cite{dtcontrol2 , while the latter issue of representing transitions not so.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In particular, we propose a new data structure to represent FSC, merging the advantages of the Mealy machine and the interpretable decision trees.
elements.sent-2411-023: The latter is used not only for representing the stationary policies similarly to literature, e.g.~\cite{dtcontrol2 , but importantly also for the automata transitions.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Besides, we identify specific properties of ``attractor-based'' policies \cite{JungesJS20 , which allow us to construct yet simpler and smaller representations with more compact and fewer transitions, by allowing skips.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our transformation procedure preserves the policies (and only changes the representation) and thus, in particular, their optimality.
elements.sent-2411-026: Further, as it is modularly defined on parts of the original policy, it explains the parts separately, simplifying the explanation compared to a larger monolyth.
elements.sent-2411-027: Partial observable MDPs (POMDPs) can be solved using various techniques including value iteration~\cite{smallwood1973 , point-based techniques ~\cite{pineau2003point , dynamic programming~\cite{geffner1998solving , Monte Carlo Tree Search~\cite{silver2010monte .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Model checkers like \storm~\cite{storm use belief-based analysis~\cite{aastrom1965optimal to find an optimal policy.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: For almost sure reachability objectives, SAT/SMT-based methods (both one-shot~\cite{almost-sure-POMDP-Kirsh2016 and iterative approach~\cite{JungesJS20 ) can be used.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Our finite state controller creation technique can take specific advantage of this iterative SMT-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-031: Decision trees (DTs) have been extensively used in operation research~\cite{von1993decision , data mining~\cite{maimon2014data and as a supervised learning framework for clustering and regression~\cite{breiman2017classification .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: They have also been suggested for representing controllers in probabilistic systems \cite{counterexample-learning , reactive synthesis \cite{viktor-reactivesynth , and hybrid systems \cite{sos-qest19 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, DTs have been used to represent and learn strategies for safety objectives by~\citet{neider-fmcad19 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Further, DTs were used for representing the policies during the model checking process, namely in policy iteration~\cite{DBLP:conf/ijcai/BoutilierDG95 or in simulation-based algorithms \cite{PnH .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The tool dtControl~\cite{dtcontrol, dtcontrol2 provides a framework for the representation of stationary policies.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Finite state controllers (FSC) have been computed in various ways, recently also synthesized in inductive synthesis approaches of \paynt~\cite{paynt or learnt by $L^*$ variant described in \cite{bork2024learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Other works have explored the idea of extracting FSCs from recurrent policy networks~\cite{rnn-2-fsc-Koul2018,rnn-2-fsc-task-aware .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Our technique takes these types of controllers as input and generates a more explainable representation.
elements.sent-2411-039: A probability distribution on a set $S$ is a function $d:S\to [0,1]$ such that $\sum_{s\in S d(S)=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We denote the set of all probability distributions on the set $S$ as $\distribution(S)$.
elements.sent-2411-041: We define the set of enabled actions in $s\in \mdpstates$ by $\mdpavailactions(s) = \{a\in \mdpactions \mid \mdptransitions(s,a) is defined\}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: A Markov chain (MC) is an MDP with $|\mdpavailactions(s)|=1$ for all $s\in\mdpstates$.
elements.sent-2411-043: For an MDP $\mdp$, a path is a sequence of states and actions.
elements.sent-2411-044: For an MDP $\mdp$, we denote the set of all finite paths by $\mdpfinitepaths$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We also assume that the observation space are factored, i.e. $ \observations = \prod_{i=1 ^{k \observations_i$ where each component $ \observations_i$ represents a distinct feature of the observation, meaning any observation $o\in \observations$ can be represented as a tuple of features $(z_1, z_2, \dots, z_k)$, where each $ z_i \in \observations_i $.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Two paths $\rho=s_0 a_0 s_1 a_1 \ldots$ and $\rho'=s'_0 a'_0 s'_1 a'_1 \ldots$ are called observation-equivalent if $\observationmap(s_i) = \observationmap(s'_i)$ for all $i$.
elements.sent-2411-047: A policy $\mdppolicy$ is deterministic if $|\supp(\mdppolicy(\mdppath))| = 1$ for all paths $\mdppath\in \mdpfinitepaths$.
elements.sent-2411-049: A policy $\mdppolicy$ is called stationary or memoryless if it depends only on the last state.
elements.sent-2411-050: We call a policy $\pi$ observation-based if for paths $\mdppath_1,\mdppath_2$, $\observationmap(\mdppath_1) = \observationmap(\mdppath_2)$ implies $\mdppolicy(\mdppath_1) = \mdppolicy(\mdppath_2)$, i.e.\ the policy has same output on observation-equivalent paths.
elements.sent-2411-051: Observation based policies resolve nondeterminism by converting a POMDP to a fully observable Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-052: This Markov chain induces a unique probability measure $\measure{\mdppolicy,s_0}$ over paths starting in initial state $s_0$~\cite[Section 10.1]{BaierKatoen .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The policy synthesis problem usually consists of finding a policy that satisfies a certain objective $\varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this paper, we consider infinite-horizon reachability or expected total reward objectives.
elements.sent-2411-055: Given a set of target states $T\subseteq S$, we denote $\measure{\mdppolicy,s_0 (\Diamond T)$ to be the probability of reaching a state in $T$ following the policy $\mdppolicy$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Also, for the set of target states $T\subseteq S$, we denote $\Expectation_{\mdppolicy,s_0 (\reward(\Diamond T))$ to be the expected total reward accumulated till we reach the states in $T$ following the policy $\mdppolicy$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In the case of almost-sure reachability objective, a policy is winning from $s_0$ if $\measure{\mdppolicy,s_0 (\Diamond T)=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Finding such a winning policy $\mdppolicy$ is EXPTIME-complete~\cite{chatterjee2010qualitative .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Alternatively, we can look at quantitative objectives: given an objective $\varphi$ and a threshold $\lambda\in(0,1)$, a policy is winning if $\measure{\mdppolicy,s_0 (\Diamond T)\geq\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: A finite memory policy is often represented by a Finite state controller as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-061: In state $s$ wth observation $z$, an agent following an FSC $\fsc$ executes the action $a = \fsclabelmap(n, z)$ and then based on the current and the next (posterior) observation $z$ and $z'=\observationmap(s')$, the FSC evolves to node $n' = \fsctransitionmap(n,z,z')$.
elements.sent-2411-062: A single-step execution of an FSC is described in \cref{alg:fsc_execution .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{A single-step execution of FSC} \label{alg:fsc_execution} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE {FSC $\fsc = (\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap, \fsclabelmap)$, POMDP $\pomdp =(\mdp, \observations, \observationmap)$, current node $n$ and state $s$} \STATE $z \gets \observationmap(s)$; $a \gets \fsclabelmap(n, z)$ \STATE Execute $a$ and transition to a state $s'$ with observation $z'$ \STATE $n \gets \fsctransitionmap(n,z,z')$; $s \gets s'$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-063: Unlike the standard definition of an FSC, transitions in this FSC are based on both the current and next observation.
elements.sent-2411-064: This `posterior-aware' FSCs have been used in literature and often give a more compact representation of controllers~\cite{andriushchenko2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: We also use this formulation since (i)~these FSCs are as expressive as the standard FSCs, and (ii) our work can handle FSCs exported by these works~\cite{JungesJS20,andriushchenko2023 and attempts to provide a more explainable representation of them.}
elements.sent-2411-066: For an input $x \in \mathbb{X}$, the output $T(x)$ is determined by evaluating the decision rules along the path from the root to a leaf, and returning the output label $a_\ell$ of that leaf.
elements.sent-2411-067: A stationary policy can be represented by a DT where the input is the set of states $\mdpstates$ and output labels describe the actions in $\mdpactions$ suggested by the policy.
elements.sent-2411-068: This is done by learning a DT from the dataset containing all the state and action pairs.
elements.sent-2411-069: In the next sections, we will describe how we can extend this idea to non-stationary policies.
elements.section-2411-003: An Illustrative Example Explaining Policy Representation
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, first, we use an example to illustrate how a non-stationary policy in a POMDP can be represented as an FSC.
elements.sent-2411-071: We will then describe how we can use the DT-based techniques to make this policy more explainable.
elements.sent-2411-072: As a running example, we use the cheese-maze model~\cite{littman1995learning as detailed in~\cref{ex:maze .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In the initial observation, a mouse is randomly put in the grid.
elements.sent-2411-074: The objective for the mouse is to reach the target (cheese) while avoiding traps placed in the grid.
elements.sent-2411-075: Each cell in the grid represents a distinct state, and the mouse can move in one of the four possible cardinal directions at each step unless there is a wall blocking it.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, the mouse's observations are limited to observing the surrounding walls, preventing it from distinguishing between cells with identical wall configurations.
elements.sent-2411-077: Consequently, there are only seven distinct observations (with the target and traps considered as separate observations).
elements.sent-2411-078: In \cref{ex:maze , the observation space of the model is structured with the following Boolean variables: CanGoDown, CanGoLeft, CanGoRight, CanGoUp describing whether the mouse can go down, left, right or up respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-079: There are two more observable variables: bad to denote whether the mouse is in a trap, clk to denote if the mouse has already been placed and goal to denote whether the mouse is in the target.
elements.sent-2411-080: In the tables, an observation is described as a vector of the values of the $7$ observable variables.
elements.sent-2411-081: For each node of the FSC, there are two tables: (i) the first table describes which action to take, and (ii) the second table describes which next node to move.
elements.sent-2411-082: In other words, the table in the nodes denotes a stationary policy that the mouse should follow initially and based on the observation, according to the transition table, it either keeps following the current policy or moves to node $n_1$ and follows the policy described as a table in node $n_1$.
elements.sent-2411-083: Instead of using large tables in every node of the FSC to define both the stationary policy and transitions, we represent them as decision trees.
elements.sent-2411-084: These DTs can leverage the structure of observations to make the representation more interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-085: Notice that, in the DTs, the predicates did not need to consider the observation CanGoUp.
elements.sent-2411-086: The representation shown in~\cref{fig:dt-fsc-maze includes two nodes and a total of four DTs: two for action selection and two for the transitions.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Compared to a traditional policy table, these decision trees provide a clearer explanation of the policy.
elements.sent-2411-088: Initially, the mouse will go up (if it is in the corridor with walls on the left and right side), and then go left.
elements.sent-2411-089: Based on the DT describing the transition relation, the mouse will switch to another stationary policy when i) it can move down but cannot move left, or ii) when it reaches a cell from which it can move down, left or right directions.
elements.sent-2411-090: After switching the policy, the mouse needs to follow the following policy based on a set of decision rules: i) if the mouse cannot move left or down, it should move right, ii) if the mouse can move down but cannot move left or right, it should move down, iii) if the mouse is in the leftmost corner of the maze, it should go right.
elements.sent-2411-091: For smaller grid-based examples, such as this one, a pictorial representation (see~\cref{fig:maze-policy in Appendix) of this winning policy using a grid would be easier to understand.}
elements.sent-2411-092: However, DT-FSC is a more general representation not bound to a specific problem domain.
elements.sent-2411-093: Additionally, the size of the grid representation of the policy would grow linearly with the size of the problem, whereas in the case of decision trees, the policy size might even remain the same in some cases.
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section, we formally outline how explainability can be integrated into an FSC using decision trees (DTs).
elements.sent-2411-095: We introduce a specific data structure called DT-FSC to facilitate this.
elements.sent-2411-096: Next, we discuss a particular scenario where FSCs (and their corresponding DT-FSCs) can be built using an iterative approach, which simplifies the explanation process.
elements.sent-2411-097: For this scenario, we also introduce a specialized data structure which provides decision-tree representations that are equally explainable but more compact.
elements.sent-2411-098: For each node in the FSC $\fsc=(\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap, \fsclabelmap)$, we can extract the corresponding stationary policy and the next transition function in tabular form.
elements.sent-2411-099: In other words, for $n\in\fscstates$, we will extract two tables: i) $\fsclabelmap_n\colon \observations\to\mdpactions$ that maps observation $z$ to the action $\fsclabelmap(n,z)$, and ii) $\fsctransitionmap_n\colon \observations\times\observations\to\fscstates$ that maps a pair of observations $(z,z')$ to the node $\fsctransitionmap(n,z,z')$.
elements.sent-2411-100: We learn decision trees $\DT_{\fsclabelmap,n}$ and $\DT_{\fsctransitionmap,n}$ which represents these functions $\fsclabelmap_n$ and $\fsctransitionmap_n$:.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{A single-step execution of DT-FSC} \label{alg:dt_fsc_execution} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE {DT-FSC $\fsc_{\DT}=(\fscstates,\fscinitstate,\{\DT_{\fsctransitionmap,n}\}_n,\{\DT_{\fsclabelmap,n}\})$, POMDP $\pomdp =(\mdp, \observations, \observationmap)$, current node $n$ and state $s$} \STATE $z \gets \observationmap(s)$ ; $a \gets \DT_{\fsclabelmap,n}(z)$ \STATE Execute $a$ and transition to a state $s'$ with observation $z'$ \STATE $n \gets \DT_{\fsctransitionmap,n}(z,z')$ ; $s \gets s'$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-101: The trees inside the nodes describe the two stationary policies.
elements.sent-2411-102: The trees below the node describe the transition between the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-103: Although the formal transition function takes both the current and the next observations as input, the decision-making may not need all these observations.
elements.sent-2411-104: Decision-tree learning is good in weeding out such observations that are irrelevant to the decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-105: For example, the decision-tree for the transition function in \cref{fig:dt-fsc-maze only requires the posterior observations, although the transition table is based on both the current and the next observations.}
elements.sent-2411-106: For the almost sure reachability objective, one can obtain FSCs using an SMT-based iterative approach as used for computing the winning region in~\cite{JungesJS20 .}
elements.sent-2411-107: In each iteration of this approach, an observation-based stationary policy is obtained that either reaches the target directly or reaches an observation from which we have already found a winning policy in a previous iteration.
elements.sent-2411-108: In the second case, the policy would switch to the previously calculated winning policy.
elements.sent-2411-109: This iterative procedure also helps us construct the FSC representing a winning policy.
elements.sent-2411-110: At iteration $i$, we add a new node $n_i$ to the FSC.
elements.sent-2411-111: The policy $\sigma_i$ found in the iteration would describe the action mapping, i.e., $\fsclabelmap(n,z)=\sigma_i(z)$.
elements.sent-2411-112: If the policy requires switching to a previously computed policy, this is captured by the transition function $\fsctransitionmap$.
elements.sent-2411-113: This can be conceptualized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-114: Based on the current observation $z$, the policy can suggest an action $a$ and does not switch to any other policy.
elements.sent-2411-115: In that case, in the constructed FSC, $\fsclabelmap(n_i,z)=a$ and the node of the FSC would not change.
elements.sent-2411-116: Upon encountering an observation $z'$, the policy can switch to a previously computed policy $\sigma_j$ (without taking any action).
elements.sent-2411-117: In that case, in the constructed FSC, for all $z\in \observations$, we would have $\fsctransitionmap(n_i,z,z')=n_j$, where $n_j$ is the node corresponding to the policy $\sigma_j$ added in the $j^{th}$ iteration.
elements.sent-2411-118: From observation $z$, the policy takes an action $a$ and then depending on the subsequent observation $z'$, it can switch to another policy $\sigma_j$.
elements.sent-2411-119: In that case, in the constructed FSC, for all $z'\in \observations$, we would have $\fsctransitionmap(n_i,z,z')=n_j$, where $n_j$ is the node corresponding to the policy $\sigma_j$ added in the $j^{th}$ iteration.
elements.sent-2411-121: The iterative procedure concludes once a winning policy is identified from the initial observations.
elements.sent-2411-122: The controller created by this approach would still have some nodes non-reachable from the initial node.
elements.sent-2411-123: We remove these irrelevant nodes by graph post-processing.
elements.sent-2411-124: For more details of the algorithm, see \cite{JungesJS20 .}
elements.sent-2411-125: Here we will give a bit more intuition about case 2.
elements.sent-2411-126: Suppose at iteration $i$, we find a policy that is winning from any state with observation $z$.
elements.sent-2411-127: Then at any later iteration, $j>i$, upon observing $z$, the agent can just switch to the previously discovered policy that is winning from $z$.
elements.sent-2411-128: This gives us the following fact: \begin{observation For any winning observation $z\in\observations$, there exists an iteration $i_z$ where we find a policy that is winning from any state with observation $z$.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Then in the FSC generated by the iterative approach, for any $j>i_z$, for any $z'\in \observations$, $\fsctransitionmap(n_j,z',z)=n_{i_z}$.. \end{observation.}
elements.sent-2411-130: We introduce an alternative representation for the FSCs produced by the iterative approach, referred to as \skippp-FSCs where we exploit the structural characteristics as described in \cref{obs:chain-FSC .}
elements.sent-2411-132: In state $s$, an agent receives observation $z = \observationmap(s)$.
elements.sent-2411-133: At current node $n$, if $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp (n, z)=\skippp$, the agent transitions to the node $n' = \fsctransitionmap_{\skippp (n,z,z)$ and continues this process until reaching a node $n''$ with $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp (n'', z)\neq\skippp$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The agent takes the action $a=\fsclabelmap_{\skippp (n'', z)$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: The state of the MDP is then changed to $s'$ according to the probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-136: Then, based on the current and the next observation $z$ and $z'=\observationmap(s')$, the FSC updates to the new node $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp (n'',z,z')$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: A single-step execution of this FSC is described in \cref{alg:skip_fsc_execution .}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{A single-step execution of a \skippp-FSC} \label{alg:skip_fsc_execution} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE {\skippp-FSC $\fsc_{\skippp}=(\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}, \fsclabelmap_{\skippp})$, POMDP $\pomdp =(\mdp, \observations, \observationmap)$, current node $n$ and observation $z$} \REPEAT \STATE $a \gets \fsclabelmap_{\skippp}(n,z)$ \STATE $n \gets \fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}(n,z,z)$ \UNTIL{$a \neq \skippp$} \STATE Execute $a$ and transition to the next state with observation $z'$ \STATE $n \gets \fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}(n,z,z')$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-138: Given an FSC $\fsc=(\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap, \fsclabelmap)$ satisfying \cref{obs:chain-FSC , we create a \skippp-FSC $\fsc_{\skippp =(\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap_{\skippp , \fsclabelmap_{\skippp )$ as described in \cref{alg:skip_fsc_creation .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: This iterates over all winning observations $z'$, modifying the transitions and action labels to incorporate the skip mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-140: Specifically, for each node $n_j$ with index greater that $i_{z'}$, the direct switching to $n_{i_{z' }$ is removed and replaced with $(j-i_{z' )$ single-step \skippp-transitions.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: We claim that the \skippp-FSC generated in this way would represent the same policy as the original FSC:.
elements.sent-2411-142: An FSC $\fsc$ generated by the iterative approach, and the \skippp-FSC $\fsc_{\skippp}$ created in \cref{alg:skip_fsc_creation from it represents the same policy.}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{\skippp-FSC representation of FSC generated by the iterative approach} \label{alg:skip_fsc_creation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE {FSC $\fsc=(\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap, \fsclabelmap)$ generated by the iterative approach} \STATE $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp} \gets \fsctransitionmap$; $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp} \gets \fsclabelmap$ \FORALL{winning observation $z'$} \FORALL{$j>i_{z'}$ and $z\in\observations$} \STATE Remove $(n_j,z,z')\mapsto n_{i_{z'}}$ from $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}$ \FORALL{$j>k>i_{z'}$} \STATE $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}(n_k,z,z')\gets n_{k-1}$ \STATE $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp}(n_k,z)\gets \skippp$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-143: For a node in the FSC, the skipping mechanism allows for a reduction in the number of possible successor nodes by replacing them with a \skipp that directly transitions to the previous node.
elements.sent-2411-144: This reduction simplifies the DTs that describe the transition function.
elements.sent-2411-145: However, the addition of the \skipp action introduces a new action label, which may slightly increase the size of the DTs describing the schedulers.
elements.sent-2411-146: Nonetheless, this does not diminish the overall explainability.
elements.sent-2411-147: Instead, the \skipp action explicitly identifies when a switch occurs, thereby providing clear information about the decision-making process and enhancing the transparency of when and why certain transitions are made.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \includegraphics[scale=1.05]{images/refuel-with-coordinates.pdf} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.4\textwidth} \fcolorbox{black}{blue!10}{ \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{images/refuel-pol6.pdf} } \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth} \fcolorbox{black}{green!10}{ \includegraphics[scale=0.1]{images/refuel-mem6} } \end{minipage} \caption{The refuel model for $6 \times 6$ grid (Left). For initial node, DT policy (Center), DT-FSC transition (Right).} \label{fig:combined-refuel}
elements.sent-2411-148: In general, policies may not be represented by finite memory controllers, e.g., when it is impossible to reach the target with probability $1$.
elements.sent-2411-149: However, one can find a policy maximizing the probability to reach the target.
elements.sent-2411-150: Unfortunately, in this scenario, the optimal policy to reach the target may need infinite memory \cite{madani2003undecidability and cannot be extracted by the iterative approach described before.}
elements.sent-2411-151: However, in practice, ``good enough'' finite memory controller policies can be designed, as done in~\cite{andriushchenko2023, bork2024learning .}
elements.sent-2411-152: In the inductive synthesis approach in \cite{andriushchenko2023 , an FSC is computed in an anytime fashion: this fixes the size of the FSC and tries to find the optimal FSC of that fixed size.}
elements.sent-2411-153: In further iterations, it increases the size and searches for a bigger but better FSC till it runs out of the given time budget.
elements.sent-2411-154: Even then, we can create a DT-FSC that explains the stationary policy defined for each node and the transition function.
elements.sent-2411-155: Since we would only have finitely many such cutoff points, there are only finitely many randomized actions suggested by the controller.
elements.sent-2411-156: Thus, we can still create a DT with finitely many leaf labels (as described in the case studies later).

elements.sent-2411-157: This benchmark consists of an \(N \times N\) grid, where a rover must travel from one corner to the diagonally opposite corner while avoiding an obstacle placed along the diagonal.
elements.sent-2411-158: The rover can observe (i)~if the current location is (start), bad, or good, (ii)~fuelmeter: a meter that provides an abstract idea of the current fuel level, (iii)~refuelStation?: if it is at a refueling station or not (i.e., it is in at a location where it can refuel), (iv)~fuelFull?: if can be refilled or not, and (v) CanGo\{Direction\} i.e., the cardinal directions in which it can move based on grid-walls.
elements.sent-2411-159: The rover can choose to move one step in any of the directions (unless there is a wall).
elements.sent-2411-160: The grid is slippery, so there is a possibility to slip making it take 2 steps.
elements.sent-2411-161: It consumes one unit of energy for each step (irrespective of it slipped or not) and can fully refill its energy at designated refueling stations located at specific coordinates within the grid.
elements.paragraph-2411-008: Explanation of DT-FSC Policies for Refuel.
elements.sent-2411-162: We explain the policy and transitions for the initial node of DT-FSC and sketch the remaining policy.
elements.sent-2411-163: Interestingly, the DT-policy in~\cref{fig:combined-refuel (Center) seems counter-intuitive at the first glance.}
elements.sent-2411-164: However, it is sound for the almost-sure-reachability objective.
elements.sent-2411-165: Let us take the starting position to be the top-left corner of the grid.
elements.sent-2411-166: The policy suggests moving in loops (south, east, north, west), with the rover refueling by playing refuel whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-167: Repeatedly cycling increases the probability that the rover will eventually reach the second refueling station $(3,3)$—a subgoal in the iterative procedure — thus ensuring success with probability $1$.
elements.sent-2411-168: The slippery nature of the grid gradually moves the rover towards the second refuel position.
elements.sent-2411-169: Once refueled at the second refuel station, the memory location switches to the next one (see the DT in~\cref{fig:combined-refuel (Right)), directing the rover to move south.}
elements.sent-2411-170: After reaching the southernmost cell, the policy then suggests the rover continue east, ultimately reaching the target while avoiding the bad state.
elements.sent-2411-171: We model the diagnosis and treatment of ischemic heart disease as described in \cite{hauskrecht2000planning .}
elements.sent-2411-172: The doctor needs to diagnose and treat patients potentially suffering from coronary artery disease (CAD) or myocardial ischemia (MI).
elements.sent-2411-173: The model accounts for cases of harmless chest pain, which do not require treatment and the risks of complications arising from diagnostic tests and treatment procedures.
elements.sent-2411-174: In this model, the actual diseases (CAD and MI) are represented by hidden state variables that are not directly observable.
elements.sent-2411-175: However, the presence of symptoms, e.g., chest pain, is observable.
elements.sent-2411-176: The doctor can use several diagnostic tools with different detection rates: an electrocardiogram (EKG) test, stress test and angiogram.
elements.sent-2411-177: The results of these tests are observable and reveal more information about the hidden state variables.
elements.sent-2411-178: For treatment, the doctor can choose between non-invasive medication, or invasive procedures like coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) and percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PCTA).
elements.sent-2411-179: CABG is generally more effective but carries a higher risk of complications, while PCTA is less invasive, but its success also depends on the presence of complications.
elements.sent-2411-180: The doctor also has the option to wait, which can resolve harmless chest pain but may lead to complications if serious conditions are left untreated.
elements.sent-2411-181: Conducting tests, treatments, and waiting incur some costs.
elements.sent-2411-182: These costs are higher if treatments are administered without proper diagnosis (i.e., without positive test results).
elements.sent-2411-183: Additional costs are incurred if complications arise.
elements.sent-2411-184: We modeled this POMDP in the \prism~language~\cite{prism and used the FSC learning technique implemented in \cite{bork2024learning to create a $4$ node FSC with the default configuration.}}
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Explanation of DT-FSC Policies for heart diseases.
elements.sent-2411-185: We provide only a partial overview of the policy here.
elements.sent-2411-186: Initially, as described in \cref{fig:dts-action-selection-heart , the policy suggests EKGTest, and depending on the results, advises whether to proceed with additional tests or medication.}
elements.sent-2411-187: It suggests going for an invasive procedure only when the stress test is positive.
elements.sent-2411-188: In the later iterations (for details, please refer to the Appendix), it suggests randomized policies in the cutoff points.
elements.sent-2411-189: While these policies can be challenging to interpret, they offer some insight into possible actions a doctor might take.
elements.sent-2411-190: For instance, if the policy assigns a higher probability to choosing pcta over medicine, then PCTA would be preferred in that scenario.
elements.sent-2411-191: Therefore, for complex models like this, a hybrid approach combining DT-FSC with human expertise can offer the needed explainability and an effective policy.
elements.sent-2411-192: We compare our approach with the one that we build on~\cite{JungesJS20 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Since the representation of policies is in different structures: tables and trees, the comparison just in terms of numbers is not valid.
elements.sent-2411-194: Nonetheless, it still gives us an impression of the difference in representation size (decision trees are supposed to be smaller than the table representation of a policy, but it is still interesting to see if the size reduction is worth the effort).
elements.sent-2411-195: Our experiment results put this information in context.
elements.sent-2411-196: We use the model checker \storm~\cite{storm to get policies using two approaches to solve POMDPs and dtControl~\cite{dtcontrol2 to get DTs.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: We implemented our prototype in Python, which uses these tools.
elements.sent-2411-198: We use the same benchmarks as used in~\cite{JungesJS20 .}
elements.sent-2411-199: It shows that our approach produces smaller representation of policies.
elements.sent-2411-200: It achieves significant reduction in size in both the stationary policies corresponding to the nodes of the FSC (which are 1.7 times larger on average) as well as the DTs corresponding to the transition functions (which are 13.5 times larger on average).
elements.sent-2411-201: The reduction in the size is much more pronounced in transition function of the FSC.
elements.sent-2411-202: Please refer to~\ref{tab:dt-fsc-vs-skip-dt-fsc and~\ref{tab:general-case in the Appendix for additional results.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: The tables for the stationary policies corresponding to the node of the FSC are partial and dependent only on current observation, whereas, the transition functions are dependent on the current and next transitions.
elements.sent-2411-204: In our experiments, the size of the table of stationary policies are much smaller than the ones for the transition functions.
elements.sent-2411-205: We think this is the reason why DTs save much more space in the transition functions than in the stationary policies.
elements.sent-2411-206: Our method profits from the graphical representation of FSCs and combines it with the DT representation of (i) the action selection in the memoryless parts of the policy and (ii) the labeling of the transitions in the FSC.
elements.sent-2411-207: Together this provides a modular and flexible framework, able to accommodate, e.g., richer transition functions (taking more observations as arguments) or richer actions (of randomized policies).
elements.sent-2411-208: Not only does it allow for better explainability, but also for better diagnostics of where the complexities arise, e.g., in which situations the second observation is really needed or under which constraints randomization occurs (and thus a validation is possibly in place).
elements.sent-2411-209: This could become even more efficient when the modeling process provides the observation and state information in a more structured way, e.g., as values of several sensors rather than an identification number.

elements.subsection-2411-007: Additional Details on Illustrative Example Section
elements.sent-2411-211: We explain the policy for maze in detail here.
elements.sent-2411-212: The numbers on the maze are to indicate different grid cells and the letters are used for observations (e.g., observation $\mathsf{b}$ means that CanGoUp and CanGoDown are true).
elements.sent-2411-213: Having same letters for cells implies that the mouse can not distinguish between those cells: e.g., $2$ and $4$ have same observation $\mathsf{y}$.
elements.sent-2411-214: Also, 6 cells of the grid ($6$-$11$) have the same observation $\mathsf{b}$.
elements.sent-2411-215: If the mouse observes $\mathsf{b}$ and can go down, it should go up to potentially avoid the traps.
elements.sent-2411-216: As soon as it sees the observation $\mathsf{g,w}$, it should move to the next policy from where there is a memoryless winning policy to reach the cheese.
elements.sent-2411-217: Observe that for observations $\mathsf{b, y}$ there are policy defined in both the memory.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/maze-mem-transitions} \caption{Policy of $n_0$ (Left) and $n_1$ (Right) in Maze and the transitions on the bold arrow. The numbers are to indicate different states and the letters are used for observations. Same letter for cells implies that the mouse can not distinguish between those states.} \label{fig:maze-policy}
elements.subsection-2411-008: Proof of Correctness for FSC with Skip
elements.sent-2411-218: For our new \skippp-FSC, we first recall the following observation.
elements.sent-2411-219: Then in the FSC generated by the iterative approach, for any $j>i_z$, for any $z'\in \observations$, $\fsctransitionmap(n_j,z',z)=n_{i_z}$.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: [H] \caption{\skippp-FSC representation of FSC generated by the iterative approach} \label{alg:skip_fsc_creation-app} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE {FSC $\fsc=(\fscstates,\fscinitstate, \fsctransitionmap, \fsclabelmap)$ generated by the iterative approach} \STATE $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp} \gets \fsctransitionmap$; $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp} \gets \fsclabelmap$ \FORALL{winning onservation $z'$} \FORALL{$j>i_{z'}$ and $z\in\observations$} \STATE Remove $(n_j,z,z')\mapsto n_{i_{z'}}$ from $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}$ \FORALL{$j>k>i_{z'}$} \STATE $\fsctransitionmap_{\skippp}(n_k,z,z')\gets n_{k-1}$ \STATE $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp}(n_k,z,z')\gets \skippp$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-220: We show that for any observation sequence $\rho$ in $\observations^*$, starting from the initial node $\fscinitstate$, both the original $\fsc$ and the $\fsc_{\skippp}$ will reach the same node and suggest same action.
elements.sent-2411-221: This is proven by induction on $|\rho|$.
elements.sent-2411-223: We stop the iteration when $\observationmap(\mdpinitstates)$ is in the winning region.
elements.sent-2411-224: So both the FSCs would suggest $\fsclabelmap(\fscinitstate,\observationmap(\mdpinitstates))$.
elements.sent-2411-225: Induction step : Assume the claim is true for some observation sequence $\rho$, meaning we reach the same node $n$ in both $\fsc$ and $\fsc_{\skippp}$.
elements.sent-2411-226: Now consider an extended observation sequence $\rho\cdot z$.. Let $\fsctransitionmap(n,\last(\rho),z)=n'$.
elements.sent-2411-227: There are two cases to consider.
elements.sent-2411-228: From \cref{alg:skip_fsc_creation-app , we have $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp (n,z)=\skippp$ for all nodes from $n$ to $n_{i_z}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Then, for the observation sequence $\rho\cdot z$, we reach the same node $n_{i_z}$ in both $\fsc$ and $\fsc_{\skippp}$.
elements.sent-2411-230: Also, as we are not adding any $\skipp$ actions from $z$ at $n_{i_z}$, we have $\fsclabelmap_{\skippp (n_{i_z ,z)=\fsclabelmap(n_{i_z ,z)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Thus, the original FSC $\fsc$ and the skip-FSC $\fsc_{\skippp}$ both reach the same node and suggest the same action for any observation sequence $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-233: Therefore, $\fsc$ and $\fsc_{\skippp}$ represent the same policy.
